era_context: The early 15th century saw the Ming Dynasty consolidating power under the Yongle Emperor, the Ottoman Empire expanding in the Balkans, the Great Schism in the Western Church creating ecclesiastical crisis, and the Hussite movement building momentum in Bohemia. The Zheng He treasure voyages were in their early phases. In the Americas, the Aztec Empire was consolidating power, and the Inca Empire was expanding.

events.title: Ming Dynasty consolidates administrative control
events.description: The Ming Dynasty under the Yongle Emperor (r. 1402–1424) was consolidating administrative control over China in 1408. The emperor had moved the capital from Nanjing to Beijing in 1421 (construction began earlier), and the empire was establishing new administrative structures and military organizations. The Yongle Emperor was at the height of his power, having defeated the Jianwen Emperor and consolidated his authority.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Yongle Emperor's consolidation of power is well-documented in Ming records; the year 1408 falls within his reign and represents a period of administrative development.

events.title: Zheng He's First Treasure Voyage returns to China
events.description: Admiral Zheng He's first major voyage of exploration, which had departed in 1405, returned to China in 1407 or 1408. The expedition had sailed to Southeast Asia, India, and the Arabian Peninsula, establishing tributary relationships and demonstrating Ming naval supremacy. The return marked the completion of a major diplomatic and exploratory mission that would be followed by subsequent voyages.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: Return date is documented in Ming records but with some uncertainty; sources place the return in 1407 or 1408, ±1 year.

events.title: Hussite movement gains momentum in Bohemia
events.description: The Hussite movement, inspired by the teachings of Jan Hus and other reformers, was gaining momentum in Bohemia in 1408. Religious and social tensions were escalating as reformist preachers challenged papal authority and ecclesiastical corruption. The movement was building support among the Bohemian nobility, clergy, and common people, setting the stage for the Hussite Wars that would begin in 1419.
events.certainty_note: The escalation of Hussite sentiment in 1408 is inferred from broader historical context; specific events for this year are not well-documented.
cross_references: 1409-003 (Jan Hus becomes rector of Charles University)

events.title: Venice and the Ottoman Empire negotiate maritime boundaries
events.description: In 1408, Venice and the Ottoman Empire were engaged in ongoing negotiations over maritime boundaries and trade rights in the Eastern Mediterranean. The two powers had competing interests in the region, and periodic conflicts and treaties characterized their relationship. By 1408, Venice was beginning to lose its dominance in the Eastern Mediterranean as Ottoman naval power grew.

sources.name: Venetian state records and diplomatic correspondence

events.certainty_note: Venice-Ottoman relations in 1408 are not specifically documented; this entry reflects the broader context of their ongoing competition.

events.title: Inca Empire expansion under Viracocha Inca
events.description: The Inca Empire under Viracocha Inca (r. ~1410–1438) was in a period of expansion and consolidation in 1408. The Inca had established control over much of the central Andes and were beginning military campaigns that would lead to the empire's greatest territorial extent under Pachacuti. Specific events for 1408 are not documented, but the empire was consolidating its power.

sources.name: Quipucamayoc (Inca record keepers) oral traditions, recorded by Spanish chroniclers
Garcilaso de la Vega, Royal Commentaries of the Incas

events.certainty_note: Inca chronology is uncertain; dates are approximate and based on later Spanish colonial records and oral traditions. Specific events for 1408 are not documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The return date of Zheng He's first voyage is disputed; sources place it in 1407 or 1408, with some uncertainty. The significance of 1408 versus 1407 is not clearly distinguished in sources. The entry on Hussite momentum in 1408 is speculative; no major events are specifically documented for this year. The entry on Venice-Ottoman negotiations reflects ongoing relations rather than a specific event in 1408. Inca chronology is highly uncertain; the assignment of Viracocha Inca's reign to ~1410–1438 is based on later Spanish colonial sources and oral traditions, and modern scholarship debates these dates significantly.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1408 is moderate but uneven. Chinese records are rich and contemporary, particularly for the Zheng He voyages, though the exact dating of voyage returns is sometimes uncertain. European events (Bohemian religious tensions, Venice-Ottoman relations) are less clearly documented for this specific year. Mesoamerican and South American history relies on post-conquest sources, introducing significant interpretive challenges and chronological uncertainty. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward elite political and military events; economic, social, and cultural history is underrepresented. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Pacific are largely absent from surviving written records.
